title: What the Body Does in the Doctor's Waiting Room
dek: You sit in the stiff plastic chair, the sterile scent of antiseptic prickling your nostrils, and the clock’s slow tick presses into your ribs. The waiting room is a crucible where your body speaks in languages you might not yet understand - the tightening of your trapezius, the hitch in your diaphragm, the clenched masseter muscle that remembers every dismissed fury. This piece unpacks what your body does in those moments, guided by somatic wisdom and the insights of teachers like Gabor Maté and Bessel van der Kolk.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Waiting Room as a Somatic Landscape


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.183Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You arrive early, the fluorescent lights humming above, the scent of Lysol and faint traces of lavender from a nearby diffuser mingling in the stale air. You settle into the plastic chair, noticing immediately how your levator scapulae muscles stiffen, pulling your shoulders up toward your ears. It’s a small, unconscious shrug - the body’s way of bracing against an invisible threat. The clock’s steady tick becomes an anchor, a metronome marking the slow crawl of time, but beneath that rhythm, your body speaks in subtle languages.

The waiting room is not just a physical space. It is a container of anticipation, vulnerability, and often, suppressed anger. You were taught that anger is ugly, something to hide or tame. Yet here, in this liminal space between illness and diagnosis, your body remembers that anger is a messenger, a body-first impulse trying to alert you to what is unseen and unspoken.

## The Jaw Remembers: Masseter Muscle and Silent Fury

Your jaw clenches without your permission. The masseter muscle tightens, a stone beneath your cheekbones. This muscle remembers every time you were silenced, every moment your fury was labeled as ugly or wrong. The tension in the masseter is a form of protest, an embodied refusal to be dismissed.

Karla McLaren, a luminary in the field of emotional intelligence and somatic awareness, teaches that anger is a guardian - a boundary setter. When your jaw tightens in the waiting room, it’s not just stress; it’s your body’s way of holding space for your anger before it can be named, before you can claim it. This tension is a signal, a way your body protects you in the face of uncertainty and the invisible dynamics of power in medical settings.

## The Diaphragm’s Hitch: Breath and the Body’s Vigil

At 3:17 pm on a rainy Tuesday, your diaphragm tugs itself up into your chest, shrinking the space for breath. The breath becomes shallow, a thin thread barely reaching the base of your lungs. This hitch in the diaphragm is your body’s primal response to waiting - waiting for news, waiting for answers, waiting for the unknown.

Pema Chödrön’s teachings on mindfulness and breathing offer a gentle invitation here. She suggests that when the breath hitches, you bring your attention gently to the sensation. You might place a hand on your belly and feel it rise and fall, even if only slightly. This conscious breath, even in the midst of tension, begins to rewrite the body’s story of waiting from one of helplessness to one of presence.

## Trapezius and Levator Scapulae: The Weight of Anticipation

Your trapezius muscles, broad and flat across your upper back, tighten like a harness pulled taut. The levator scapulae, those thin muscles connecting your neck to your shoulder blades, twist and knot, a physical manifestation of the ache in your heart. This is not just stress; this is the body carrying the weight of anticipation and fear.

Gabor Maté, whose work on trauma and somatic awareness is profound, reminds us that the body does not lie. When your trapezius and levator scapulae muscles are clenched, it is the body’s way of holding the tension of past wounds alongside present anxieties. This tension is not pathology; it is biology, a language of survival and protection.

## The Body’s Smell: The Scent of Stress and Herbal Remedies

The waiting room smells sterile, but your body carries its own olfactory signature in this moment. There is the faint scent of sweat - salt and musk - hidden beneath your skin, a primal marker of your nervous system’s activation. You might catch a hint of your own rosemary and lavender-infused body oil, an herbal talisman against the sterile atmosphere.

Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) teaches that the lungs and large intestine govern grief and letting go, but also that the liver is the seat of anger. When you feel your body’s tension rise, herbs like [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) or [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help modulate your nervous system’s fight-or-flight response. These herbs are not magic; they are allies, working with your body’s rhythms to restore balance in moments where waiting feels like a battle.

## The Clock’s Tick and Time as a Body Experience

Time slows in the waiting room. The clock’s tick-tock is not just a sound but a somatic experience that presses against your ribs, each second a drop of pressure. It is 4:05 pm, and the light outside has shifted from harsh to dim, the sky a slate gray.

Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist focused on racialized trauma, writes extensively about how bodies carry histories and traumas in muscles and fascia. In the waiting room, the clock becomes a symbol of the body’s endurance. The fascia, that connective tissue webbing your muscles together, holds tension accumulated not only from this moment but from years of taught silence and suppressed fury.

## Embodied Practice: Releasing the Jaw and Inviting Breath

You can try this now. Place your fingertips gently on your masseter muscles, just above the angle of your jaw. Notice the hardness there, the tightness like a stone. Begin to breathe deeply, sending your breath down into your belly. As you exhale, allow your jaw to soften, the masseter releasing its grip.

Imagine the breath traveling down through your diaphragm, expanding the space in your chest. Feel your trapezius and levator scapulae loosen, shoulders dropping away from your ears. This is not about forcing relaxation but about inviting your body to remember its capacity for release, even in the crucible of waiting.

## The Body Knows: Trusting Somatic Wisdom in Medical Spaces

In the doctor’s waiting room, your body is a repository of knowledge. It remembers the times you were told to be quiet, to hide your anger, to smile through pain. It carries the biology of waiting, the muscle memories of fear, and the somatic signals of unspoken fury.

Bessel van der Kolk, whose work on trauma therapy is foundational, emphasizes that healing happens when the body is allowed to speak. Your tension is a language, your breath a narrative. Waiting is never passive; it is a deeply embodied experience that calls for your attention and respect.

Honor what your body is doing in the waiting room. This is not pathology. This is biology. This is your body’s way of telling you that it knows, it remembers, and it is still here.

## FAQ

**Q: Why does my jaw clench when I’m waiting for the doctor?**

**A:** The masseter muscle tightens as a response to stress and suppressed anger. Your body is holding space for your feelings, even if you haven’t named them yet. This clenching is a form of somatic protection.

**Q: How can I calm my breath when I feel it hitching in anticipation?**

**A:** Bringing your attention gently to your breath and placing a hand on your belly can help. Try to lengthen the exhale, inviting your diaphragm to expand slowly. This practice is about presence, not forcing relaxation.

**Q: Are herbs effective for managing waiting-room anxiety?**

**A:** Herbs like Ashwagandha and Skullcap can support your nervous system’s regulation, but they work best as allies alongside somatic awareness and breath work. Always consult with a healthcare provider before starting any herbal regimen.

**Q: Is my tension in the shoulders and neck just stress, or something more?**

**A:** This tension is a somatic memory of both current and past experiences of waiting, fear, and suppressed anger. It’s your body’s biology, not simply stress, carrying a history that deserves attention and care.

**Q: How can I use what I’ve learned here in other moments of waiting?**

**A:** Notice the specific muscles that tighten, bring mindful awareness to your breath, and consider herbal allies if appropriate. Recognize that your body is communicating, and responding with curiosity rather than judgment is a powerful act of self-care.
